name: garage door spring failure - full prose with image_observations
raw: {"thought":"The first image clearly shows the left torsion spring is absent from its bracket while the right spring is intact and seated correctly. The bracket on the left retains only the mounting collar, with no visible spring coil between it and the centre cone. This asymmetry — one spring present, one missing — is the primary fault. The second image corroborates this by showing the connecting rod bent at the midpoint, which is consistent with the door being repeatedly cycled with one spring counterbalancing the full weight. No alternative cause (cable failure, broken roller, motor fault) is visible in either frame.","diagnosis":"Broken Torsion Spring","message":"The left torsion spring on your garage door has snapped and is no longer counterbalancing the door weight. With only the right spring carrying the load, the connecting rod has bent at the midpoint because it is taking forces it was never designed to absorb on its own.\n\nDo not operate the door — even by hand — until the spring is replaced. A door with one broken spring can drop unpredictably and the motor can be damaged if forced.","action_required":"","contractor_checklist":["Replace both torsion springs as a matched pair, not just the broken one.","Inspect and straighten or replace the bent connecting rod.","Balance the door manually after the new springs are installed.","Check the cable drums and lift cables for collateral damage."],"homeowner_prep":"Keep the door closed and unplug the motor at the wall socket until the contractor arrives.","image_descriptions":["Left torsion spring is missing from its bracket; only the mounting collar remains. The right spring is present and visually intact.","Connecting rod is bent at approximately its midpoint, with visible deformation along the shaft."],"image_observations":[{"primary_observation":"Left torsion spring is missing from its bracket; only the mounting collar remains.","components_visible":["left bracket","right torsion spring","centre cone"],"components_missing_or_damaged":["left torsion spring (absent, only bracket remains)"],"role_in_diagnosis":"primary_evidence"},{"primary_observation":"Connecting rod is bent at approximately its midpoint.","components_visible":["connecting rod","shaft brackets"],"components_missing_or_damaged":["connecting rod (bent at midpoint)"],"role_in_diagnosis":"corroborating"}],"clarification_questions":[],"diy_verification":"With the motor disengaged, try lifting the door by hand; if it feels twice as heavy as you remember and immediately wants to drop, the spring balance is gone.","photo_request":"","confidence_drivers":["Clear view of asymmetric spring assembly","Bent rod visible in corroborating image","Description matches the visible damage"]}
